---
title: 'CLIP: Learning Transferable Visual Models from Natural Language Supervision'
description: Paper notes on CLIP — contrastive image–text pretraining on 400M pairs
  for open-vocabulary, zero-shot vision.
categories:
- Multimodal
---

Paper: [arXiv:2103.00020](https://arxiv.org/abs/2103.00020)

## 1. Introduction and Motivating Work

NLP has been revolutionised by large-scale raw-text pre-training. This natural language supervision is powerful for open vocabulary and zero-shot learning. Vision, however, has not caught up because it still relies on fixed-label supervised data. Early natural-language-supervised models were too small or narrow; CLIP scales up contrastive learning on 400M pairs, enabling open-vocabulary, zero-shot vision.

## 2. Approach

### 2.1. Natural Language Supervision

Different papers use different names for image–text pair training; it does not fall into standard ML categories. The authors suggest the term *natural language supervision*, which treats text as the supervisory signal for vision models. Earlier models such as bag-of-words, n-grams, or topic models limited the amount of information extracted from captions; now, thanks to models like ELMo, BERT, and GPT, text encoders are powerful enough to turn free-form language into meaningful vectors. Natural language supervision is advantageous in several ways: it is scalable, as the data already exist on the internet as captions, hashtags, and so on, and it ties the representation to language, making it useful for zero-shot transfer.

### 2.2. Creating a Sufficiently Large Dataset

The problem with existing datasets is that they are either too small, or big but noisy. To solve this, the authors build their own large-scale dataset called WIT (WebImageText). Starting with all words that appear more than 100 times in English Wikipedia, augmented with bi-grams, they created a base list of 500k queries. For each query they searched for images with natural-language text. To avoid overloading common words, they limited each query to at most 20,000 (image, text) pairs. The dataset sums to 400M (image, text) pairs.

### 2.3. Selecting an Efficient Pre-Training Method

State-of-the-art vision models were extremely expensive. The first approach — training a CNN (image encoder) and a transformer (text encoder) jointly to predict the caption words given the image — was very inefficient and slow. Both bag-of-words prediction and full caption prediction share a flaw: predicting the exact words of the text. Descriptions from the internet are noisy, so predicting exact words slows down learning. Prior work showed that contrastive objectives learn better representations than predictive ones.

Instead of predicting which words appear, the authors predict which text matches which image. Given a batch of $N$ (image, text) pairs, Contrastive Language–Image Pre-training (CLIP) computes similarity scores for all $N \times N$ possible pairings; real pairs have high cosine similarity, while wrong pairs have low similarity. The model is optimised using a symmetric cross-entropy loss over these similarity scores. As the dataset is large, overfitting is not an issue, so the authors train the image and text encoders from scratch instead of using pre-trained weights. They dropped the non-linear projection head and use only a linear projection to the multi-modal embedding space. Text handling and image augmentation are simplified, and the temperature parameter is learnable.

### 2.4. Choosing and Scaling a Model

The authors used two image encoders: ResNet-50 and ViT. For ResNet-50, the final global average pooling is replaced with an attention-pooling layer so the model focuses more on important regions. Traditional scaling just increased depth or width, but the authors adopted EfficientNet-style compound scaling. For the text encoder, they used a transformer; the image encoder dominates, so text-encoder scaling is simpler.

### 2.5. Training

- Epochs: 32
- Batch size: 32768
- Image encoder: ResNet-50 (×4, ×16, ×64), ResNet-101, ViT-B/32, ViT-B/16, ViT-L/14
- Optimisation: AdamW
- Learning rate: cosine decay
- Hyperparameter tuning: grid search, random search, manual tuning on ResNet-50 trained for 1 epoch
- Contrastive temperature: initialise $\tau = 0.07$; clip to prevent scaling the logits by more than 100

## 3. Experiments

### 3.1. Zero-Shot Transfer

#### 3.1.1. Motivation

In CV, zero-shot learning usually means training on some categories and testing on new ones. CLIP expands the meaning to generalisation to unseen *datasets*. Each dataset can be viewed as a proxy for a task. Most unsupervised / self-supervised work measures the quality of representations; CLIP focuses on task learning — the representation is tied to language, which lets it directly perform tasks, and zero-shot transfer is then a natural measure of capability. On such benchmarks, zero-shot transfer tests robustness. The most related prior research is Visual N-Grams, which performed zero-shot transfer with a pre-trained vision–language model. CLIP adopts zero-shot transfer as evidence of learned task capabilities, not just features.

#### 3.1.2. Using CLIP for Zero-Shot Transfer

Passing the image through the image encoder generates a vector $f(x)$, and passing each candidate class name (converted into a sentence prompt) through the text encoder generates $\{ g(c_{i}) \}$. Cosine similarity is computed by

$$
s_{i} = \frac{f(x) \cdot g(c_{i})}{\Vert f(x) \Vert \, \Vert g(c_{i}) \Vert}
$$

Scale the similarities by the learned $\tau$ and apply softmax across all classes:

$$
p(c_{i} \mid x) = \frac{\exp(\tau s_{i})}{\sum_{j} \exp(\tau s_{j})}
$$

This is mathematically equivalent to multinomial logistic regression with L2-normalised features and classifier weights. The image encoder can be interpreted as the backbone that extracts features; the text encoder is a hypernetwork that generates the weights of the classifier from class names. Every CLIP batch can be viewed as a mini-dataset with 32768 classes, each defined by one text string — so training CLIP is essentially equivalent to solving these mini zero-shot classification problems.

#### 3.1.3. Initial Comparison to Visual N-Grams

Visual N-Grams was an early but limited attempt at zero-shot learning. CLIP pushes this into a practical regime: accuracy is high enough that zero-shot becomes a real alternative to supervised classifiers.

#### 3.1.4. Prompt Engineering and Ensembling

CLIP requires language descriptions of classes, but most datasets only store numeric label IDs with a file mapping them to English words. A common challenge is polysemy, where one word has multiple meanings; another is the mismatch with pre-training data, since CLIP's captions are rarely a single word. To bridge the gap, the authors use the prompt template "A photo of a {label}", which adds minimal natural-language context. Another trick is to build multiple zero-shot classifiers from different prompt variations. Normally, ensembling runs each classifier and averages their probability distributions, which is expensive. Instead, CLIP averages the text embeddings first and normalises to a unit vector, so at inference one compares the image embedding with a single averaged vector per class.

#### 3.1.5. Analysis of Zero-Shot CLIP Performance

Zero-shot CLIP is competitive with a supervised linear probe on many natural-vision tasks, but falls behind on domains far from the internet photo/caption distribution or requiring specialised signals. A 4-shot linear probe is roughly similar to zero-shot CLIP, because zero-shot CLIP's head is generated by language and directly encodes the concept, removing ambiguity that a small example set cannot resolve. Since the zero-shot head is linear, a fully supervised linear probe on the same features offers a rough upper bound. Zero-shot ability scales predictably with compute.

### 3.2. Representation Learning

The goal of CLIP is task-agnostic, dataset-agnostic pre-training. Fine-tuning gives higher accuracy but masks weaknesses in the pre-trained representation; a linear probe shows how much structure the representation already contains. Linear-probe evaluation shows CLIP's representations are competitive with or better than supervised ImageNet models.

### 3.3. Robustness to Natural Distribution Shift

CLIP features transfer more robustly than ImageNet features, and zero-shot CLIP is more robust under natural shifts. Adapting CLIP to ImageNet boosts in-distribution accuracy but hurts average performance under natural distribution shift; from zero-shot to few-shot, the robustness fades.

## 4. Comparison to Human Performance

CLIP is much better than humans at zero-shot. Humans bring strong priors, unlike CLIP's few-shot methods, which do not integrate prior knowledge.

## 5. Data Overlap Analysis

CLIP is trained on 400M internet (image, text) pairs, and some evaluation datasets may contain internet photos too, leading to data leakage. Removing all overlaps before training would require knowing all evaluation datasets in advance; instead, CLIP trains on everything and measures overlap afterward:

1. For each evaluation dataset, run a nearest-neighbour duplicate detector and use a threshold to split it into Overlap, Clean, and All.
2. Compute CLIP's zero-shot accuracy on all three splits.
3. Run a binomial significance test.

## 6. Limitations

- Performance gap with state-of-the-art models
- Weakness on certain tasks (fine-grained)
- Out-of-distribution generalisation: CLIP assumes future data look like the training data
- Limited zero-shot interface: it cannot generate new labels or descriptions
- Poor data efficiency
- Biases from internet data
- Few-shot performance drop

## Code

```python
class CLIP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self,
                 embed_dim: int,
                 # vision
                 image_resolution: int,
                 vision_layers: Union[Tuple[int, int, int, int], int],
                 vision_width: int,
                 vision_patch_size: int,
                 # text
                 context_length: int,
                 vocab_size: int,
                 transformer_width: int,
                 transformer_heads: int,
                 transformer_layers: int
                 ):
        super().__init__()

        self.context_length = context_length

        if isinstance(vision_layers, (tuple, list)):
            vision_heads = vision_width * 32 // 64
            self.visual = ModifiedResNet(
                layers=vision_layers,
                output_dim=embed_dim,
                heads=vision_heads,
                input_resolution=image_resolution,
                width=vision_width
            )
        else:
            vision_heads = vision_width // 64
            self.visual = VisionTransformer(
                input_resolution=image_resolution,
                patch_size=vision_patch_size,
                width=vision_width,
                layers=vision_layers,
                heads=vision_heads,
                output_dim=embed_dim
            )

        self.transformer = Transformer(
            width=transformer_width,
            layers=transformer_layers,
            heads=transformer_heads,
            attn_mask=self.build_attention_mask()
        )

        self.vocab_size = vocab_size
        self.token_embedding = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, transformer_width)
        self.positional_embedding = nn.Parameter(torch.empty(self.context_length, transformer_width))
        self.ln_final = LayerNorm(transformer_width)

        self.text_projection = nn.Parameter(torch.empty(transformer_width, embed_dim))
        self.logit_scale = nn.Parameter(torch.ones([]) * np.log(1 / 0.07))

        self.initialize_parameters()

    def build_attention_mask(self):
        # lazily create causal attention mask, with full attention between the vision tokens
        # pytorch uses additive attention mask; fill with -inf
        mask = torch.empty(self.context_length, self.context_length)
        mask.fill_(float("-inf"))
        mask.triu_(1)  # zero out the lower diagonal
        return mask

    def encode_image(self, image):
        return self.visual(image.type(self.dtype))

    def encode_text(self, text):
        x = self.token_embedding(text).type(self.dtype)  # [batch_size, n_ctx, d_model]
        x = x + self.positional_embedding.type(self.dtype)
        x = x.permute(1, 0, 2)  # NLD -> LND
        x = self.transformer(x)
        x = x.permute(1, 0, 2)  # LND -> NLD
        x = self.ln_final(x).type(self.dtype)
        # take features from the eot embedding (eot_token is the highest number in each sequence)
        x = x[torch.arange(x.shape[0]), text.argmax(dim=-1)] @ self.text_projection
        return x

    def forward(self, image, text):
        image_features = self.encode_image(image)
        text_features = self.encode_text(text)

        # normalized features
        image_features = image_features / image_features.norm(dim=1, keepdim=True)
        text_features = text_features / text_features.norm(dim=1, keepdim=True)

        # cosine similarity as logits
        logit_scale = self.logit_scale.exp()
        logits_per_image = logit_scale * image_features @ text_features.t()
        logits_per_text = logits_per_image.t()

        return logits_per_image, logits_per_text
```
